nativeRange.habitat: Small tributaries, creeks, pools, and seasonally flooded forest areas with slow-moving, warm water

diet.notes: Readily accepts sinking pellets and wafers as a staple; benefits from supplementation with frozen or live foods such as bloodworm, Daphnia, and Artemia. Scavenges the substrate — feed sinking foods to ensure they receive adequate nutrition.

summary: A popular warm-water Cory with distinctive spotted patterning and orange-tinted fins. Tolerates temperatures up to 30°C, making it a compatible bottom-dweller for Discus and Ram tanks. Best kept in groups of 6 or more on a fine sand substrate.
careNotes: One of the most popular Corydoras species in the hobby, distinguished by its bold spotted/reticulated pattern and orange-hued pectoral fins. Unlike most Corydoras, C. sterbai tends to tolerate and even prefer warmer water (24–30°C), making it well-suited for tanks housing Discus or German Blue Rams — tankmates that typically require elevated temperatures. An albino variant is widely available commercially and is hardier than many albino morphs. Sand substrate is strongly preferred over gravel; fine substrate protects the sensitive barbels from injury and infection. Undergravel filters are generally considered incompatible with this species as they can damage the barbels and cause infections. Copper-based medications have been reported to be harmful and should be avoided; use alternative treatments when disease arises. Facultative air-breathers — they have been observed gulping air at the surface, which is normal behavior. Paraguay has been cited as an additional range country by some sources, though the core documented range is the Rio Guaporé basin in Brazil and Bolivia. Note on taxonomy: FishBase (2024) lists this species under the synonym Hoplisoma sterbai, though Corydoras sterbai remains the universally used aquarium trade name.
breedingNotes: Generally considered one of the more accessible Corydoras species to spawn. Egg-scatterers that deposit adhesive eggs on flat surfaces and plants. Breeding tends to be triggered by a simulated rainy season — cool partial water changes, increased feeding of live or frozen foods, and slightly elevated temperatures are commonly reported triggers. Warmer water (26–28°C) is typically maintained during conditioning. Fry are small and benefit from infusoria or micro-worm as first foods. Breeding difficulty has been described as moderate given that while they will often spawn naturally, raising fry successfully requires attention to water quality and appropriate first foods.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish
primary.notes: Primary species profile. Provided size (6.5 cm), pH (6.0–7.6), hardness (1–15°H), native range (Bolivia/Brazil, Rio Guaporé), habitat description, min tank dimensions (45×30×30 cm), and compatibility notes.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked scientific name (also listed as Hoplisoma sterbai), taxonomy (Callichthyidae / Siluriformes), max size (6.8 cm SL), pH (6.0–8.0), hardness (2–25 dH), and native range (central Brazil and Bolivia). FishBase temperature of 21–25°C reflects cooler wild habitat data; aquarium-hobby sources consistently recommend 24°C minimum for captive care, and upper bound of 30°C supported by warm-water association with Discus tanks.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op
additional.notes: Confirmed warm-water tolerance and Discus/Ram compatibility. General corydoras range given as 72–82°F (22–28°C); sterbai specifically noted as tolerating higher temperatures. Also confirms group-of-six recommendation and sinking-food diet. pH given as 6.6–8.0 (broad trade range).

fish.breedingNotes: Egg-scatterers; warmer-water and cool water-change triggers commonly reported. Fry require infusoria or micro-worm as first foods.
